title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about astronomy and the human fascination with space exploration. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Looking Outward
content.subtitle: An article about humanity's enduring relationship with the night sky.
content.text:
For as long as people have looked upward, the stars have prompted both wonder and questions. Ancient civilisations built monuments aligned with celestial events, charted the movements of planets, and wove the night sky into their myths. Yet what began as observation driven by curiosity and the need to mark seasons has become, in the modern era, an ambition of staggering scale. [31] The decision to send humans to the Moon in the 1960s was not primarily a scientific one — it was a political statement made at the height of a global ideological contest. Science, in that instance, was the means, not the end. [/31]

The legacy of that era, however, proved unexpectedly rich. Technologies developed for space travel — from lightweight materials to water purification systems — filtered into everyday life in ways their inventors never anticipated. [32] Critics who questioned the cost of space programmes rarely accounted for these indirect benefits, which are notoriously difficult to quantify but have touched virtually every industry. [/32] A kidney dialysis machine, for example, owes a debt to NASA engineering; so does the scratch-resistant coating on your reading glasses.

Today, space exploration is undergoing a profound transformation. For decades it was the preserve of governments; now private companies are competing to launch satellites, carry tourists beyond the atmosphere, and eventually establish permanent settlements on other worlds. [33] Proponents argue that commercialisation will accelerate progress and reduce costs through competition. Sceptics, however, worry that profit motives may divert attention from scientific goals and that the benefits will be concentrated among the already wealthy. [/33]

Among the most compelling current endeavours is the search for signs of past or present life beyond Earth. Mars has long been the focus of such hopes, partly because its geological history suggests it once held liquid water. [34] Yet scientists are careful to temper public enthusiasm. The detection of biosignatures — chemical indicators that might point to biological processes — would represent a statistical suggestion rather than a confirmation, at least at first. [/34] The leap from 'this could be life' to 'this is life' is enormous, and the history of science is littered with premature announcements.

Perhaps more than any practical application, it is the philosophical dimension of space exploration that gives it lasting power. [35] The overview effect — the cognitive shift experienced by astronauts who see Earth from a distance — has been described by those who have experienced it as a fundamentally altering encounter with one's own insignificance. [/35] Several astronauts have returned convinced of the need for greater environmental stewardship and international cooperation, struck by how fragile and borderless their planet appeared from orbit.

Whether driven by competition, commerce, or curiosity, humanity's outward gaze shows no sign of fading. [36] The universe, it seems, will always be large enough to accommodate our ambitions and to remind us, at the same time, of how little we have yet understood. [/36]

questions.question: What point does the author make about the decision to send humans to the Moon?
options: A) It was inspired mainly by the scientific discoveries of the time.
B) Its primary motivation was not scientific advancement.
C) It was widely criticised as a waste of resources.
D) It was the result of cooperation between rival nations.
questions.explanation: The author explicitly states it 'was not primarily a scientific one' but rather 'a political statement made at the height of a global ideological contest'.

questions.question: The author mentions kidney dialysis machines and scratch-resistant coatings in order to
options: A) show how space technology was deliberately adapted for medical use.
B) illustrate the difficulty of measuring the value of space research.
C) give concrete examples of indirect benefits from space programmes.
D) suggest that everyday inventions are more useful than spacecraft.
questions.explanation: These examples are given immediately after the claim that indirect benefits 'have touched virtually every industry', serving as concrete evidence for that point.

questions.question: Which concern do sceptics of commercial space exploration raise?
options: A) Private companies lack the technical expertise of government agencies.
B) The cost of space tourism will prevent scientific missions from launching.
C) The scientific agenda may be compromised by the desire for profit.
D) Competition between companies will result in dangerous shortcuts.
questions.explanation: The text states sceptics 'worry that profit motives may divert attention from scientific goals'.

questions.question: When discussing the search for life on Mars, the author implies that scientists
options: A) are confident that life once existed there.
B) believe biosignatures are more reliable than other evidence.
C) want to avoid raising unrealistic expectations.
D) have already detected signs of biological processes.
questions.explanation: Scientists are described as careful to 'temper public enthusiasm', and the text warns against 'premature announcements', indicating caution about raising unrealistic expectations.

questions.question: What does the author say about the 'overview effect'?
options: A) It is a psychological condition that affects some astronauts negatively.
B) It has caused astronauts to abandon their careers in space.
C) It results in a changed perspective on Earth and human responsibility.
D) It only occurs when astronauts travel beyond the solar system.
questions.explanation: The text says astronauts return 'convinced of the need for greater environmental stewardship and international cooperation', showing a changed perspective on Earth.

questions.question: The final paragraph suggests that space exploration
options: A) will eventually answer all of humanity's fundamental questions.
B) continues to inspire despite reminding us of our limited knowledge.
C) is becoming less relevant as other global challenges grow.
D) depends entirely on maintaining commercial investment.
questions.explanation: The closing sentence balances human ambition with the reminder of 'how little we have yet understood', suggesting ongoing inspiration alongside humility.
